verses.commentary: Do you not know, brothers and sisters, for I am speaking to those who know the law, that the law has authority over someone only as long as that person lives? Paul addresses those familiar with legal principles. Law binds only the living. Death severs legal obligations. This sets up the marriage analogy.

For example, by law a married woman is bound to her husband as long as he is alive, but if her husband dies, she is released from the law that binds her to him. Marriage law illustrates the principle. Death breaks the marriage bond. The wife is legally free when her husband dies.

So then, if she has sexual relations with another man while her husband is still alive, she is called an adulteress. But if her husband dies, she is released from that law and is not an adulteress if she marries another man. Adultery is a legal category while the husband lives. Death changes the legal status. She is free to remarry without guilt.

So, my brothers and sisters, you also died to the law through the body of Christ, that you might belong to another, to Him who was raised from the dead, in order that we might bear fruit for God. The application: we died to the law through union with Christ's death. Now we belong to the risen Christ. The purpose: to bear fruit for God. The law could not produce fruit, but union with Christ does.

For when we were in the realm of the flesh, the sinful passions aroused by the law were at work in us, so that we bore fruit for death. In the flesh refers to life before regeneration. The law aroused sinful passions (it revealed and provoked sin). The result was fruit for death. The law exposed sin but could not overcome it.

But now, by dying to what once bound us, we have been released from the law so that we serve in the new way of the Spirit, and not in the old way of the written code. Released from the law does not mean lawlessness but a new relationship. Service shifts from external code to internal Spirit. The Spirit writes God's law on the heart (Jeremiah 31:33).

What shall we say, then? Is the law sinful? Certainly not! Nevertheless, I would not have known what sin was had it not been for the law. For I would not have known what coveting really was if the law had not said, You shall not covet. The law is not sin, but it reveals sin. Paul uses the tenth commandment as an example. The law exposes internal sin (coveting), not just external acts.

But sin, seizing the opportunity afforded by the commandment, produced in me every kind of coveting. For apart from the law, sin was dead. Sin uses the law as an opportunity (aphormē, ἀφορμή, a base of operations). The commandment provokes rebellion. Apart from law, sin lies dormant (dead does not mean non-existent but inactive).

Once I was alive apart from the law; but when the commandment came, sin sprang to life and I died. Alive apart from the law likely refers to childhood innocence or life before moral awareness. The commandment awakened consciousness of sin. Sin sprang to life, and spiritual death resulted. The law brings awareness of death, not life.

I found that the very commandment that was intended to bring life actually brought death. The law's purpose was to show the path to life (Leviticus 18:5). But because of sin, the law became an instrument of death. The law is good, but we are not.

For sin, seizing the opportunity afforded by the commandment, deceived me, and through the commandment put me to death. Sin deceives by using the good law to provoke rebellion. Deception echoes Eden (Genesis 3:13). Sin turns God's good gift (the law) into a weapon against us.

So then, the law is holy, and the commandment is holy, righteous and good. Paul vindicates the law. The problem is not the law but sin. The law reflects God's character: holy, righteous, good. It is a perfect standard.

Did that which is good, then, become death to me? By no means! Nevertheless, in order that sin might be recognized as sin, it used what is good to bring about my death, so that through the commandment sin might become utterly sinful. The law is not the problem; sin is. Sin's perversity is exposed by using something good (the law) to kill. The law magnifies sin's sinfulness. Sin becomes utterly sinful (kath' hyperbolēn, καθ' ὑπερβολήν, beyond measure) when it turns God's law against us.

We know that the law is spiritual; but I am unspiritual, sold as a slave to sin. The law is spiritual (pneumatikos, πνευματικός), originating from the Spirit. But I am fleshly (sarkinos, σάρκινος), dominated by the flesh. Sold as a slave to sin depicts total bondage. The conflict is between the spiritual law and the fleshly self.

I do not understand what I do. For what I want to do I do not do, but what I hate I do. Internal conflict defines the human condition under sin. The will is divided. Wanting good but doing evil reveals moral inability. This is the anguish of knowing what is right but being powerless to do it.

And if I do what I do not want to do, I agree that the law is good. Doing what I hate proves the law is good. The law's verdict is confirmed by my own judgment. The problem is not the law's standard but my inability to meet it.

As it is, it is no longer I myself who do it, but it is sin living in me. This is not excusing sin but diagnosing it. The true self desires good; indwelling sin produces evil. There is a distinction between the regenerate self and remaining sin. This is the tension of already-not yet: justified but not yet glorified.

For I know that good itself does not dwell in me, that is, in my sinful nature. For I have the desire to do what is good, but I cannot carry it out. No good dwells in the flesh (sarx, σάρξ, the sinful nature). The will desires good, but the power to perform is absent. This is moral impotence.

For I do not do the good I want to do, but the evil I do not want to do, this I keep on doing. The repetition intensifies the cry. The pattern is persistent: desiring good, doing evil. Keep on doing (present tense) indicates ongoing struggle.

Now if I do what I do not want to do, it is no longer I who do it, but it is sin living in me that does it. The distinction reappears: the regenerate I versus indwelling sin. This is not denial of responsibility but recognition of the internal enemy. The battle is within.

So I find this law at work: Although I want to do good, evil is right there with me. A law (principle) is discovered: wanting good does not eliminate the presence of evil. Evil is right there with me (parakeimai, παράκειμαι, lying beside). The proximity of evil is constant.

For in my inner being I delight in God's law. Inner being (esō anthrōpos, ἔσω ἄνθρωπος, inner man) is the regenerate self. Delight in God's law shows a transformed heart. This is Psalm 1:2 lived out. The regenerate person loves God's law.

But I see another law at work in me, waging war against the law of my mind and making me a prisoner of the law of sin at work within me. Two laws (principles) at war: the law of the mind (agreeing with God's law) and the law of sin (indwelling corruption). War language (antistrateuomai, ἀντιστρατεύομαι) depicts intense conflict. Prisoner status shows the power of indwelling sin.

What a wretched man I am! Who will rescue me from this body that is subject to death? A cry of desperation. Wretched (talaipōros, ταλαίπωρος) means miserable, afflicted. The body of death (tou sōmatos tou thanatou, τοῦ σώματος τοῦ θανάτου) is this mortal body marked by sin and death. The question expresses helplessness and longing for deliverance.

Thanks be to God, who delivers me through Jesus Christ our Lord! The answer: deliverance comes through Jesus Christ. The cry of despair turns to thanksgiving. Christ is the rescuer. So then, I myself in my mind am a slave to God's law, but in my sinful nature a slave to the law of sin. The tension remains until glorification. The mind serves God's law; the flesh still struggles with sin. This is the Christian's present reality: justified, being sanctified, awaiting glorification.
